content: Hypertensive emergency management: ICU admission, continuous BP monitoring (arterial line preferred), IV antihypertensives. General goal: reduce MAP by no more than 20-25% in first hour, then to 160/100-110 over next 2-6 hours, then gradual normalization over 24-48 hours. Exceptions: aortic dissection (SBP <120 within 20 minutes, HR <60), acute ischemic stroke (permissive HTN unless thrombolysis planned—then <185/110), intracerebral hemorrhage (SBP 140-160), ACS (reduce myocardial oxygen demand). Preferred agents: nicardipine (calcium channel blocker—titratable, cerebral/coronary vasodilation), clevidipine (ultra-short acting CCB), labetalol (combined alpha/beta blocker—avoid in asthma, heart block), esmolol (short-acting beta-1 selective—good for aortic dissection), nitroglycerin (venodilator—preferred for ACS, pulmonary edema), nitroprusside (arterial/venous dilator—avoid in renal impairment, risk of cyanide toxicity), fenoldopam (dopamine agonist—renal vasodilation), hydralazine (unpredictable, reflex tachycardia—avoid except in pregnancy). Avoid sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable, risk of stroke/MI).
